data.title: Politics And The Markets 06/14/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Americold Realty Vs. Lineage: It's A Cold World
data.description:
Today, we are going to dive head first into an asset class where the best laid plans of mice and men went awry. We are going to discuss one of the best positioned asset classes which quickly ran into trouble following the pandemic. No, we are not going to be talking about still-troubled office space or poorly occupied enclosed shopping malls.

Rather, the purpose of this article is going to be focusing on something much more niche. We are going to discuss one of the most misunderstood asset classes, cold storage. Cold storage happens to be one of the most complex corners of real estate, requiring significant capital outlay and ongoing CapEx to maintain modernity. While other industrial assets are extremely economically efficient with low cost to build and maintain, cold storage sits on the other end of the spectrum. These behemoth assets are power hungry and require a significant amount of upkeep to keep running.

Despite difficulties around the inputs of cold storage, the asset class is projected to remain remarkably hot over the next decade. According to supply chain research groups, demand for cold storage space is anticipated to continue growing. According to research from Skyquest, the global cold storage market was valued at just over $140 billion in 2024. It is expected that over the next 10 years, the value of global cold storage could surpass $500 billion. This growth is largely due to modernization of global food distribution, and the increasing need for last mile and localized food logistics. This corresponds to a market compound annual growth rate of more than 17% over the next decade.

The growth of cold storage has been noteworthy over the past 10 years. While the asset class remains minimally popular due to the complexities, a significant number of real estate investors have actually begun to touch cold storage. For example, Realty Income (O) invested in a series of cold storage deals around the pandemic, including this one in Garner, NC which traded for more than $40 million.

By all accounts, the asset class has been hot, with demand projected to continue an extraordinary growth trajectory. With a recipe for success, cold storage was anticipated to be all the rage over the past five years. However, these expectations have largely fallen short due to a combination of factors outside of the complexities that I mentioned previously.

The purpose of this article is to discuss the two largest publicly traded cold storage REITs, explain the difficult market, and provide an outlook of which is more appealing. Lineage (NASDAQ:LINE) and Americold (NYSE:COLD) are the two largest cold storage REITs with nearly 5 billion cubic feet of combined cold storage capacity, far ahead of other market participants.

Let’s provide an overview of each REIT before diving into the market update and comparison.

Who Is Americold?

COLD is the world’s second-largest owner and operator of cold storage facilities and temperature controlled warehouses. The specialized REIT has a portfolio of around 240 cold storage warehouses located around the world.

Of these assets, the vast majority are operated by COLD with only three managed by third-party operators. In totality, the company’s portfolio corresponds to nearly 1.5 billion cubic feet of storage capacity. COLD owns nearly 200 facilities throughout North America, with an additional 25 properties in Europe and 16 across the Asia Pacific region. The company's most recent expansions include developments in New Zealand and Australia, where exports of animal products are growing rapidly to support international markets including China. Where food demand goes, COLD follows. In the European region, the company has begun to acquire assets throughout the United Kingdom and the Netherlands. When acquiring properties, the company strategically focuses on assets that are close to infrastructure, driving long-term demand. These infrastructure hubs can include ports, airports, or major rail lines which can provide food distribution. The company's business model includes the acquisition of assets for long-term hold, as well as the ongoing development and sale of new assets. The company even has a history of redeveloping existing assets for cold storage use.

The cold storage industry emphasizes vertical integration, where operators of warehouses offer value add services such as transportation management and packing/inventory management. This means that companies like cold have revenue drivers outside of rent. From one perspective, this adds economic efficiency as additional revenue drivers are added to the business period, from another perspective, this presents operational risk that can become more apparent during volatile periods. Over the past several years, cold has emphasized automation and technological integration to their platform, including their warehouse management systems, or WMS, initiative.

COLD has a massive balance sheet that includes investment grade credit ratings from major agencies. As of Q1, the company had more than $500 million in available liquidity. Total debt outstanding was nearly $4 billion, which included well laddered maturities. Leverage is relatively low for the business, with a net debt to EBITDA ratio of just under 6x. The vast majority of outstanding debt is unsecured and fixed rate at 95% and 86%, respectively. The company's weighted average interest rate is around 4% and there are no maturities remaining for the current year. The company's weighted average debt maturity is around 5.1 years.

Who Is Lineage?

Now let's go ahead and switch our focus to the largest competitor in the cold storage industry. While cold is the oldest publicly traded real estate operator in the cold storage segment, another participant has recently emerged. LINE went public roughly 12 months ago, following years as a privately backed cold storage fund. As the industry boomed, the company went public, allowing the fund to recapitalize and reintroduce itself to the public.

As of the end of Q1, LINE had a portfolio of nearly 500 cold storage warehouses around the world. The company's footprint covers more than 3 billion cubic square feet of storage capacity around the globe. North America accounts for the largest segment of the portfolio, with nearly 225 assets across 37 U.S. states and Canada. As a newer company, LINE’s portfolio is newer and has an increased emphasis on technology as compared to COLD.

LINE also follows an infrastructure-centric approach, seeking assets that are close to vital transportation hubs. This includes roughly half of the portfolio being close to ports, a vital food distribution mechanism. LINE has an impressive international footprint, with more than 19 countries in the portfolio.

From a balance sheet perspective, these two companies are similar. LINE also has investment grade credit ratings for major rating agencies.

What Are The Key Differences?

When it comes to picking apart different REITs within a concentrated industry, the differences between the two will often become more nuanced. As illustrated above, there are overlapping similarities between these two real estate companies. At the highest level, both own a similar lineup of internationally diversified cold storage facilities, but let’s find some differences.

LINE is considerably larger than COLD with approximately double the overall storage capacity. This provides a scale advantage compared to their competitor, which can help provide cost efficiencies. This difference is key for a real estate industry which is considerably more cost intensive than other segments.

COLD is a much older company, and many of the assets they own are older as well. LINE discusses an emphasis on technology, which includes enhancing the efficiency of acquired assets. The technological integration associated with the company’s cold storage platform accounts for cost efficiencies as compared to competitors in the broader market, as well as COLD. COLD has been public since 2018 while LINE has a track record of less than one year as a public company.

Importantly, LINE became a critical competitor for COLD. By adding another player to the cold storage industry, it became easier to value COLD and assign a multiple. Today, LINE trades at a forward FFO multiple of 21x compared to 16x for COLD. Since IPO, the market has assigned LINE a valuation premium based on the operational efficiencies and more aggressive growth trajectory.

What Happened To Cold Storage?

Before a verdict, let’s zoom out and talk about the industry more broadly. I mentioned that the industry witnessed a post-pandemic boom followed by a bust. As the market began to demand food distributors to provide a more efficient home delivery model, cold storage assets became a focal point of the supply chain. Building out a more comprehensive global logistics platform of temperature controlled warehouses became an essential portion of delivering food directly to homes, rather than a consolidated grocery store footprint. From some standpoints, the trends have continued, with companies such as Amazon (AMZN) continuing to invest in their home delivery platforms for groceries.

Between 2020 and 2023, the market delivered nearly six million square feet of speculative cold storage space. The wave of deliveries created oversupply throughout the market, forcing vacancy rates up, net absorption down, and ultimately put downward pressure on rents. The dramatic shift in cold storage space and demand also led to an interesting phenomenon where third-party logistics companies like Flexcold emerged to occupy these speculative developments. The new developments within the industry have introduced a degree of tumult.

Historically, developers built cold storage assets in isolated areas where land was cheap, electricity was readily available, and zoning rules were few and far between. However, the post pandemic landscape forced developers to reconsider this strategy as consumers began to demand these cold storage assets were placed closer to city centers. The emergence of infill cold storage became a considerable cost burden for developers who were building these speculative assets. In an effort to avoid these increasing costs, these market pressures also led to other changes, including the proliferation of multi-story cold storage.

Companies like LINE and COLD are some of the most prolific builders during this period, leading the charge with the massive construction boom around the world. Since, these companies have felt the burden and their stock price has become reflective of the troubles within the market. As with many booms came the inevitable bust and cold storage has since began to withdraw following several years of extraordinary growth. The stock prices of COLD and LINE reflect this decline.

Looking forward, issues continue to plague the industry. Emergent trends like tariffs and labor uncertainties have added fuel to the fire. Cold storage continues to reel and over the past 12 months, both of these REITs have continued to see their stock prices pressured. Today, developers have all but abandoned the speculative development model and are now focusing nearly exclusively on build to suit projects for existing tenants. This trend is likely reflective of a cooling around the 3PL cold storage model.

Who Is Better Positioned To Succeed?

These two companies are remarkably similar in that they are both the largest cold storage operators in the world. In an industry without many participants, the initial public offering of line several years ago marked an important introduction of a competitor for cold. From a scale perspective, both of these REITs are large, with more than a billion cubic square feet of capacity. Cutting through the noise and focusing on the more nuanced differences becomes important when picking apart two companies such as these.

COLD takes a more traditional approach to the business. The company has a slower growth trajectory and focuses more on the core cold storage business model. The company is also older with an older portfolio of assets but possibly a more established client list. Finally, COLD has taken up a much more conservative growth trajectory than LINE, who has emphasized acquisitions and development to become the largest cold storage REIT in the world.

In contrast, LINE takes a more modern approach, hoping to integrate their business deeper into a client model. This means revenue sources other than rent are a more important piece of their business than COLD. This also accounts for the premium valuation offered to LINE versus COLD. The valuation difference also corresponds to differences in dividend yield. Today, LINE pays a 4.66% dividend, compared to 5.18% for COLD.

As both companies continue to struggle amid cold storage headwinds, I find COLD’s more traditional business model to be more appealing than LINE. I see opportunity within the industry as long-term headwinds remain strong. In the meantime, supply chain uncertainties, rising costs, and other emergent risks have continued to pressure the industry. COLD earns a Buy rating, while LINE earns a Hold rating.

data.title: Pinterest: Rebound Opportunity As User Momentum Regains Steam
data.description:
In today’s stock market, I’m increasingly concerned about simply investing in index funds that are dominated by large-cap growth stocks. To me, beating the markets in this expensive environment requires careful stock-picking, and identifying assets that have their own independent catalysts for growth and can buck the broader economy.

Pinterest (NYSE:PINS), in my view, is one of those stocks. The social media company, best known for its interest-driven pins and boards, is a particularly appealing option after a ~20% slide over the past year (though the stock has begun a fierce rebound since late April).

Now, do note that I’ve been either cautious or bearish on Pinterest for quite some time. Last quarter in March, I upgraded Pinterest to a buy rating based on improving user trends and an appealing valuation. Now, after the company’s Q1 earnings release showed a continuation of progress (particularly on user statistics), I’m reiterating my buy rating on this company.

To me, there are a number of reasons that Pinterest truly stands out as a top pick in the internet/social media space:

  • Pinterest has reinvigorated user growth in the U.S., which peers have struggled with. Though Pinterest faces saturation in the core North American market like most of its competitors, the company has returned to growth in the U.S. while others like Snap are seeing declines.
  • Progress in international monetization. While the average revenue per user (ARPU) in most overseas markets is still tiny compared to the U.S., the company continues to make consistent progress in boosting ARPU in these markets where user growth is more pronounced.
  • Differentiated content. Much like Reddit (RDDT), another social media company that I view as having differentiated content, Pinterest’s organization around interests distinguishes the company from more personal content/video narratives that Snap, TikTok, and Meta all compete in.
  • Economies of scale. Pinterest continues to consistently expand its adjusted EBITDA margins as it keeps a careful lid on opex growth.

And despite this year’s rebound, I continue to find that Pinterest is still trading at buyable levels. At current share prices near $35, Pinterest trades at a market cap of $22.90 billion. After we net off the $2.61 billion of cash (against zero debt, another reason to be confident in Pinterest) on Pinterest’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $20.3 billion.

Meanwhile, for the current year FY25, Wall Street analysts are expecting Pinterest to generate $4.16 billion in revenue, or 14% y/y growth. If we conservatively assume a 29% adjusted EBITDA margin on that revenue profile (flat to the company’s TTM margin and not considering any further expansion), adjusted EBITDA would be $1.21 billion. This puts Pinterest’s valuation multiples at:

  • 4.9x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 16.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA

Here’s how these multiples stack up against various social media and internet peers:

I appreciate the fact that Pinterest trades at lower EBITDA multiples than Snap, especially given Pinterest's better user trends in North America. The stock's cheaper revenue multiples versus Meta (META) are also promising, though we note that Pinterest will likely never be able to eclipse Reddit's premium valuations, given the latter's premium growth rates.

To me, Pinterest remains a very differentiated asset that is quietly building up user strength and monetization while bolstering its bottom line. This certainly isn’t a “hold forever” stock - no social media company is, given the speed of trend changes. But I believe that in the short to medium term, investors can capitalize on Pinterest’s advantages to earn a decent above-market return.

Q1 download

Let’s now go through Pinterest’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Pinterest’s revenue grew 16% y/y to $855.0 million, beating Wall Street’s expectations of $846.1 million (+14% y/y) by a two-point margin. We note straight off the bat as well that international revenue growth far outstripped the company total, giving us plenty of hope in the company’s progressing monetization in overseas regions. Revenue in Europe grew 24% y/y to $147 million, and revenue in the “rest of the world” excluding Europe and North America grew 49% to $45 million.

To me, however, the most important indicator is the fact that Pinterest’s user trends regained momentum. Global users added 17 million sequentially versus Q4 (most of which were centered in the "rest of world" region) and grew 10% y/y. Meanwhile, monthly active users in the U.S. and Canada also returned to growth, adding 1 million in the quarter and growing 4% y/y.

This is where we can draw meaningful compares versus several of Pinterest's peers. Though Meta no longer reports daily or monthly active users by region (only reporting a "daily active people" metric for the entire globe), we note that it does report ad impressions per region, which is a useful proxy for user activity. Ad impressions in North America decelerated to just 4% growth (in line with Pinterest's MAU growth in the region), and decelerated 2 points sequentially from 6% growth in Q4.

Snap, meanwhile, fared even worse: in the most recent quarter, Snap lost 1 million users sequentially and declined -1% y/y in the U.S.:

And though the U.S. still represents the lion's share of Pinterest's revenue, we like the fact that average revenue per user in Europe advanced 17% y/y to $1.00 and in the rest of the world 27% y/y to $0.34 (though we note the latter still has a long way to catch up to a >$6 value per user in North America).

In particular, Pinterest is driving higher advertiser adoption via rolling out its lower funnel marketing playbook, which was successful in the U.S., to other markets. These initiatives capitalize well on Pinterest's interest and product-focused social media platform, which are naturally conducive to online shopping. Per CEO Bill Ready's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

For markets outside of the U.S. and Canada, we are successfully exporting our lower funnel shopping playbook and increasing actionability. Revenue from our catalog, shopping ads format was up meaningfully in Q1 year-over-year with both the Europe and rest of world regions growing shopping ad revenue over 3x faster than the overall revenue growth of their respective regions, as we drive deeper budget penetration in these markets and unlock new accounts focused on performance.

International revenue is also benefiting from the scaled solutions we've unlocked over the past year, including resellers and other demand partnerships. In Q1, we expanded our reseller efforts to eight additional markets, while making strong progress through these reseller partnerships in previously un-monetized or under monetized countries."

We appreciate that in spite of improved monetization and user growth, Pinterest managed also to cut down corporate overhead, reducing G&A spending by -4% y/y to $70 million (8% of revenue, 2 points lower than 10% in the year-ago quarter).

Driven primarily by this reduction, Pinterest's adjusted EBITDA grew 37% y/y to $172 million, representing a 20% adjusted EBITDA margin that advanced 3 points y/y.

Risks and key takeaways

We should, of course, be cognizant of the risks that Pinterest faces. Pinterest is certainly not immune to a weakening global macroeconomy: amid tighter consumer spending, advertisers may also pull back on their marketing budgets, which may hurt the company's recent trend of ARPU expansion. Investors should also be wary of the fact that social media is an ever-changing landscape, and the threat of a new entrant that draws away eyeballs from Pinterest is ever-present (especially as existing social media rivals integrate more commerce features into their platforms).

That being said, I find the opportunity to invest in Pinterest at a ~18x adjusted EBITDA multiple (despite a >30% pace of adjusted EBITDA growth) to be quite attractive and compensate for these Risks. As long as the company can manage to maintain steady user growth in the U.S. (the market that generates three-quarters of overall revenue), I'm bullish and comfortable holding on to Pinterest as it scales its bottom line.

data.title: Protara Therapeutics: Marching On To A Hopefully Eventful Back Half Of 2025
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I last covered the cancer drug developer Protara Therapeutics (NASDAQ:TARA) back in December 2024, after a then-recent spike based on positive data readout in bladder cancer, I was optimistic about the investment thesis. So far, 2025 has not lent strength to that optimism. Today, I want to take another look as they've put a few more quarters under the belt, in addition to new data updates.

Pipeline Updates

TARA-002

TARA currently has one clinical oncology-focused pipeline candidate is TARA-002, a powdered bacterium that is intended to work similar to Bacillus Calmette-Guerin (BCG), a standard treatment option for patients with high-risk, non-metastatic bladder cancer. The primary driver of developing a novel agent that acts like BCG is the ongoing shortages of BCG, which crimps availability of this important treatment option.

When last we left TARA-002, it was with the rather spectacular positive readout of an ongoing phase 2 trial called ADVANCED-2, showing a 6-month complete response rate of 72% (70% at any point during follow-up), and activity was noted even in patients who had received prior BCG therapy. As a reminder, this was similar to the complete response rate demonstrated by Anktiva when combined with BCG in patients with BCR-unresponsive, non-muscle invasive bladder cancer.

Since then, clinical development has been rather quiet. There was an update of findings from ADVANCED-2 at the AUA Annual Meeting back in April. Among patients with BCG-unresponsive disease, all 5 had a complete response to treatment with TARA-002. In a BCG-naive cohort, 16 of 21 (76%) achieved a complete response at any time. The company guided in the announcement that interim results of 25 patients with a six-month evaluation would come by the end of the year. They are also in conversation with the FDA about the design of a pivotal trial in BCG-naive disease, which they intend to communicate more details of in the second half of 2025.

TARA-002 is also being explored in patients with lymphatic malformations, a rare disease of benign growths that occur during fetal development. TARA has various designations from the FDA and EC for this disease, and the ongoing STARBORN-1 trial is continuing to enroll pediatric patients, and an interim update is expected in the second half of 2025.

THRIVE-3

TARA is also deep in clinical investigation of what they hope will be the first intravenous formulation of choline for patients undergoing parenteral support. This is planned to be evaluated in the phase 2b/3 THRIVE-3 trial versus placebo. This agent has a Fast Track designation with the FDA.

Financial Overview

As of their latest quarterly filing, TARA held $91.5 million in cash and equivalents, as well as another $32.9 million in marketable debt securities. They also had $33.2 million in non-current securities. Operating expenses reached $14.1 million, and after interest, the net loss for the quarter was $11.9 million.

Net cash used for the quarter reached $14.7 million, for an estimated annualized negative cash flow of $58.8 million. This suggests that TARA maintains a cash runway of approximately 2.5 years, consistent with management guidance that their liquidity should be sufficient to fund operations into 2027.

Strengths and Risks, Revisited

Strength - The seeds of something very promising in bladder cancer

Company updates related to the ongoing clinical trial describing the use of TARA-002 have been slow to emerge. In 2025, their interim data readouts have only added a few patients here and there, with hopefully a more robust readout coming later this year.

But, what little they have added has been encouraging. With small clinical trials, the general rule is "regression to the mean," where highly favorable response rates (or whatever measure of efficacy you're following) tend to fall to a more reasonable level as you add more patients to the study.

Right now, TARA-002 is holding up as far as these early signs of activity are concerned.

Risk - These are still early days for TARA-002

That said, I would still not get too far ahead of myself in speculating on the impact of TARA-002 in these early-stage bladder cancer patients. These are still small patient numbers, and there is still room for future readouts to make the activity look modest.

Strength (soon) - Capital raise eliminates near-term cash concerns

Liquidity was substantially improved with a capital raise tied to the initial promising readout of their clinical study for TARA-002. It's always encouraging to see these things conducted from a relatively solid position of strength. With well over 2 years of cash in the tank, TARA can drive pretty far into clinical development and give a clearer picture of the future of TARA-002 or IV choline before needing to tap the well again.

Bottom-Line Summary

TARA had a very exciting end to the year 2024, and 2025 has so far been relatively quiet. It would appear as though the decline in share price since my last article on the company could be explained by "market boredom," for lack of a better phrase. TARA has not let loose with a trickle of empty news reports. Rather, they appear to be aligning themselves to present data when they're going to offer as much information as possible.

Despite the decline since my initial "Buy" sentiment, I remain confident that there is an interesting thesis here. It is high-risk/high-reward, of course, but I don't see anything clinically that gives me pause at this time about TARA-002. It will be critically important to watch as this story develops, though.

Moreover, the cash position is stronger than when I last covered them, with years of runway now eliminating the risk of needing to find dilutive financing from a position of weakness. I continue to like the nascent story here, and I think it continues to be worthy of a "Buy" sentiment.

data.title: 7 Upcoming Dividend Increases
data.description:
Companies that consistently increase their dividends are often viewed as wonderful investments for the long haul, showcasing their strong financial health and stability. Regular dividend boosts suggest that a company is generating impressive cash flow, providing both income and total returns for investors to enjoy!

I keep a close eye on dividend increases from strong companies and other important data points. I've compiled this information as part of my investment strategy, and I’m thrilled to share my insights with my Seeking Alpha community. These lists are crafted to help you in making informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll highlight more companies and present additional information about them, as well as the methodology behind the selection.

Here are some highlights about the group this week:

  • The average increase is 7.6%, and the median is 5.6%.
  • The average streak length is 16 years

My investment approach centers around buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that both pay increasing dividends and can also provide better-than-average returns (determined by beating a relevant benchmark).

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining two data sources: the “U.S. Dividend Champions” spreadsheet and upcoming dividend data from NASDAQ. This combination meshes data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company in this list has at least 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories, as I'll use them throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10–24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 0
Champion 1
Contender 4
Challenger 2

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Donaldson Company, Inc. (DCI) 39 1.72 16-Jun-25 11.11% Champion
Chesapeake Utilities Corporation (CPK) 22 2.29 16-Jun-25 7.03% Contender
UnitedHealth Group Incorporated Common Stock (UNH) 16 2.78 16-Jun-25 5.24% Contender
First Financial Bankshares, Inc. (FFIN) 15 2.07 16-Jun-25 5.56% Contender
Enterprise Financial Services Corporation (EFSC) 11 2.24 16-Jun-25 3.45% Contender
Garmin Ltd. Common Stock (Switzerland) (GRMN) 8 1.73 16-Jun-25 20.00% Challenger
Vistra Corp. (VST) 6 0.51 18-Jun-25 0.67% Challenger

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date you need to own the stock.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
DCI 0.27 0.3 11.11%
CPK 0.64 0.69 7.03%
UNH 2.1 2.21 5.24%
FFIN 0.18 0.19 5.56%
EFSC 0.29 0.3 3.45%
GRMN 0.75 0.9 20.00%
VST 0.22 0.23 0.67%

Additional Metrics

Some different metrics related to these companies include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52-Week Low 52-Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
DCI 69.59 57.45 78.95 27.02 21% Off Low 12% Off High
CPK 120.49 102.64 136.73 25.66 17% Off Low 12% Off High
UNH 318.18 248.88 630.73 19.98 28% Off Low 50% Off High
FFIN 36.66 27.62 44.66 26.84 33% Off Low 18% Off High
EFSC 53.47 37.31 63.13 12.56 43% Off Low 15% Off High
GRMN 208.39 158.38 246.5 22.8 31% Off Low 16% Off High
VST 173.86 66.5 199.84 10.34 161% Off Low 13% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the “Chowder Rule,” the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
UNH 2.78 11.7 13.2 14.2 18.8 17
CPK 2.29 8.5 10.1 9.6 9 11.9
EFSC 2.24 10 12.2 10.8 18 13
FFIN 2.07 0 6.3 8.5 9.9 10.6
GRMN 1.73 2.8 3.8 5.7 4.6 7.4
DCI 1.72 8 7.1 5.2 5.1 6.9
VST 0.51 31.2 21.1 16.6 17.1

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD boasts a strong performance record, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $68B in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four main factors in the index that SCHD tracks.

Based on this analysis (and further due diligence), I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

I'm excluding VST as they don't have a 10-year dividend growth rate, but I'll compare everyone else against SCHD.

For reference, SCHD returned 182% over the past decade.

This week, we have a few great headlines. First, we have the dramatic fall from grace of UnitedHealth. Missed earnings, pulled guidance, leadership issues, and ongoing investigations into business practices spell disaster for a once-darling of the DGI community. Prior to the recent pullback, this was a high flyer of a total return stock. Still, even after the 40% pullback, it's 202% has bested SCHD.

On the other hand, Garmin has been a darling of late, offering investors a total return of over 515% in the past decade. Though much of that has come in the past two years, its previous peak had put it over the top of UNH back in the craziness of 2021. Last I recall of Garmin, they sold the standalone GPS units we all had in the early aughts. Nevertheless, the business has managed to transition and shareholders have been handily rewarded.

Now, after those, there are not a lot of compelling investment ideas from the rest of the bunch. I will say, everyone performed approximately in the realm of what SCHD did, save for DCI. But, their nearly 130% total return has bested many other companies in the weekly reviews that I perform.

Next Steps

I tend to be quite selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. I'm on the lookout for companies that not only provide significant alpha over SCHD but really stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. While I do enjoy having a personal interest in the companies I invest in, having a personal relationship isn't strictly necessary. These articles give me (and I hope you) a wonderful chance to review what’s performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings as they share their annual updates.

Given that, I'm not interested in any company this week. For-profit health insurance is not my bailiwick, and I left my feelings for Garmin back with my standalone GPS device before smartphones took over the world.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

The Dividend Champions list is a monthly compilation of companies which have consistently increased their annual dividend payouts. However, since this list is only produced once per month, the data in it can quickly get out of date. data.title: Tiziana: Scope Of Foralumab Goes Beyond That Of Multiple Sclerosis Targeting
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Tiziana Life Sciences (NASDAQ:TLSA) it was with respect to a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Tiziana: Foralumab Drug Development Presses On With Additional EAP Enrollment". With respect to this article, I noted that the company was working on advancing the use of its fully human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody foralumab for the treatment of patients with non-active secondary progressive multiple sclerosis [naSPMS] This is being done so in an open-label trial Expanded Access [EA] program. It was shown that patients given this drug over a 6-month period either saw an improvement or stabilization of their disease.

Not only that, but that the company is already in the process of developing this anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody drug in a phase 2a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study targeting these patients with naSPMS. This time around, I believe it is important to go over another promising program from the company's pipeline, which is that this fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody can also be developed to target patients with mild-cognitive impairment due to early-stage Alzheimer's Disease [AD].

More about this below, but the key is to target the microglial activation that causes inflammation found in these patients with this progressive neurodegenerative disorder. Why do I believe it is important to bring this up? Well, it is because there is a catalyst approaching in the near-term, which would be the initiation of a phase 2 study using foralumab to treat these patients with early-stage AD at some point in 2025. As far as the phase 2a randomized double-blind study goes, using this drug to target patients with naSPMS, data from this trial is expected to be released in 2026. However, the CEO is expected to present recent clinical findings for this MS treatment program at the Bio International Convention on June 18th 2025 at 11:45 AM ET.

A Second Indication To Explore With Foralumab On The Horizon

As I noted above, the company is in the process of running both its open-label Expanded Access [EA] program and its phase 2a randomized, double-blind study using foralumab to treat patients with non-active secondary progressive multiple sclerosis [naSPMS]. The beauty of this anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody is that it could be used to target a multitude of other neurodegenerative and neuroinflammatory disorders beyond that of only Multiple Sclerosis [MS]. With that being said, the company is diving the use of developing intranasal foralumab to target patients with mild cognitive impairment with early-stage Alzheimer's Disease. Before going over the expected trial in detail, plus any catalysts to come about from this specific program, it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for Tiziana Life Sciences could end up being. Alzheimer's Disease [AD] is a type of neurodegenerative disorder whereby there is a progressive loss of nerve cells [neurons] in the brain due to neurofibrillary tangles in the brain. Plus, there is a buildup of amyloid plaques, which cause brain cells to die off. In essence, the brain cells being lost causes the brain to shrink and leads to inflammation as well. With that being said, this disorder can cause memory loss, behavior issues and thinking. The global Alzheimer's Disease market is expected to reach $23.8 billion by 2031. This is a very large market opportunity for the company to go after.

In order to see whether or not fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody intranasal foralumab can be used effectively to target mild-cognitive impairment due to early-stage AD, the company is expected to initiate a phase 2 randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to assess the safety and efficacy of this drug for the treatment of these patients. What is known about the study itself is that it is listed as "not yet recruiting" because the study has not yet begun. The company believes that it is expected to begin this phase 2 study at some point during this year in 2025. Specifically, this study is expected to randomize patients 3:1 to receive one of the following doses:

  • 50 micrograms of nasal foralumab
  • 100 micrograms of nasal foralumab
  • Placebo

It is important to note that one of these forms of treatments is to be given in three-week "treatment cycles". In essence, the goal is to give three doses of this drug per week for 2 weeks and then have 1 week as a rest period. Two important primary outcome measures being looked at are adverse events [AEs] and microglial activation observed with PET scan imaging from baseline to 20-weeks.

Why the need for the latter efficacy endpoint, I noted? Before going into this, it is first important to note what I stated above, which is that AD is caused due to several factors, which are:

  • Amyloid plaques
  • Neurofibrillary tangles
  • Microglial activation

The last one noted, microglial activation, is what causes inflammation or disease burden in patients with Alzheimer's Disease [AD]. Thus, the point of ameliorating disease burden is to reduce the amount of microglial activation that takes place. Then, how does foralumab as a fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody help in this regard? It does so by modulating regulatory T-cells to dampen microglial inflammation. The potential is to reduce inflammation to the point that a patient doesn't have a lot of microglial activation in place to affect their thinking, memory and behavior. This factor of reduced microglial activation ameliorating disease burden of AD was shown in a murine model. Not only was this shown, but it was done so independent of amyloid beta deposition.

Thus, not only can the company initiate a phase 2 study to use foralumab to treat these patients with early-stage AD, but it can do so in combination with an anti-amyloid therapy as well. The bottom line is that it can go beyond that of only using this intranasal anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody alone to target neuroinflammation associated with AD. Speaking of the intranasal route of administration, the Tregs are modulated locally; thus there is no systemic toxicity observed in using foralumab to inhibit microglial activation because of the route of administration being done. What remains to be seen now with the soon-to-be initiated phase 2 randomized, double-blind study is that the theory of using Tregs to modulate microglial activation does result in reduced inflammation. Thus, in tandem, brings about improved cognition for these patients with early-stage AD. With all this being said, the company believes that it can initiate the phase 2 randomized study, using foralumab to treat these AD patients, at some point during this year in 2025.

Financials

According to the 20-F/A SEC Filing, Tiziana Life Sciences had cash and cash equivalents of $3.72 million as of December 31st of 2024. It doesn't have a lot of cash on hand, it is highly likely that it will need to raise funds at least at some point this year. The reason why I state this is because in its 20-F/A SEC Filing, it notes that it believes it has enough to fund its operating expenses and capital expenditures for the immediate future. Not only that, but it lists a risk that it may be forced to scale back its operations if necessary to conserve cash and carry its pipeline forward. This would likely end up involving to halt further development of one of its ongoing studies to conserve cash. It is burning through a lot of cash each year; thus it is going to need to continue raising capital as often as it can through progression of its studies. For the year ending December 31, 2024 it burned through 15.79 million in cash. This is broken down into $5.22 million in R&D expenses and then $10.56 million in operating expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Tiziana Life Sciences. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the development of the ongoing phase 2 study, using foralumab for the treatment of patients with naSPMS. The company is expected to report top-line data from this mid-stage trial at some point in 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the results to be released from this study are going to be positive for starters. Nor, that the company will be able to receive authorization from regulators to move this program towards the next stage of clinical testing.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the soon-to-be initiated phase 2 study using foralumab for the treatment of mild cognitive impairment in patients with early-stage Alzheimer's Disease [AD]. Upon initiation, along with eventual completion of this trial, there is no assurance that the mechanism of action [MOA] of locally inducing Tregs [T-regulatory cells] will result in the control of microglial activation in the brain. The hope is that an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody given via an intranasal route of administration results in disease reduction burden for these patients with AD.

The third and final risk to consider would be the company's cash position. As I laid out above, it doesn't have enough cash on hand, given the fact that it only ended December 31st of 2024 with only $3.72 million in cash. With that being said, it is highly likely that it is going to need to raise cash in the next several months. This is going to be through the use of equity offerings, as noted in the 20-F/A SEC Filing. Plus, there is also the risk noted that it could possibly scale backs its operations. This would be to reduce ongoing studies or halt development of specific clinical drugs from its pipeline.

Conclusion

Tiziana Life Sciences is continuing to progress the use of its fully-human anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody foralumab for the treatment of patients with naSPMS. This program entails the advancement of two ongoing studies, which are the open-label Expanded Access [EA] program trial and the phase 2 randomized, double-blind placebo controlled one. It is not much longer to see the completion of the mid-stage randomized study because the projection is that the company could release data from it at some point in 2026.

One near-term catalyst to focus on though would be the initiation of a phase 2 study using foralumab to treat patients with mild cognitive impairment due to early-stage AD. This is expected to begin shortly, and the hope is that the company establishes proof-of-concept in using an intranasal anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody like foralumab effectively uses Tregs to modulate microglial cells.

Plus, you also have the upcoming presentation of prior data released by the CEO of this drug targeting patients with naSPMS. Such results will be shown at the upcoming Bio International Convention to be held on June 18, 2025 at 11:45 AM ET. With mid-stage trial data expected from foralumab program targeting patients with naSPMS in 2026, plus the initiation of a phase 2 study targeting patients with early-stage AD on the way, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: RCI Hospitality: Several Concerning Trends To Note (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
RCI Hospitality Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:RICK), the nightclub operator and Bombshells restaurant chain owner, has reported concerning earnings. Nightclubs have shown weaker sales due to several transitory factors, but decreasing alcohol consumption also acts as a more persistent threat to RCI Hospitality’s core business. At the same time, Bombshells’ slight turnaround signals have faded.

I remained at a Buy rating in my previous January article on the stock, titled “RCI Hospitality: Back To Basics”. The stock has since returned -22%, underperforming the S&P 500’s return of 5%.

RCI Hospitality’s Performance Is Weakening

RCI Hospitality’s fiscal Q2 report from early 2025 showed quite a subdued performance. Both the nightclub segment and Bombshells showed concerning earnings trends, albeit some of the reasons for the performance are transitory.

The nightclub segment had a suddenly weaker performance. Total segment revenues were reported at $57.5 million, down by -3.1% year-on-year due to a noteworthy -3.5% decline in same-store sales. As a result of weaker revenues, segment earnings also declined. Nightclubs’ adjusted operating income came in at $17.1 million for the quarter, down by -13.5% year-on-year through sales deleverage and other negative factors. Overall, earnings still stayed at a healthy level with a 29.8% operating margin, but the decline in earnings is notable regardless for a historically quite stable segment.

Some of the segment decline is due to transitory reasons, not being too concerning over the long term. Other entertainment venue operators also reported weak traffic for the period, as I have written on companies such as Topgolf Callaway (MODG) and Lucky Strike (LUCK) (articles: MODG, LUCK). Consumer confidence has been lower throughout the period, causing more cautious consumer activity. On top, traffic had a hiccup due to weak weather during the quarter, and a fire in RCI Hospitality’s Fort Worth location caused further disruption to the revenue performance. Restaurant chains’ traffic also reflects a weak nationwide trend in especially February. The mentioned factors should ultimately turn out temporary as consumer confidence should eventually rebound, and as weather conditions normalize and operations are restored in Fort Worth.

The nightclub segment’s performance does also warrant some caution over the longer term. Notably, alcohol sales declined by -5.3% for Q2, being a critical part of the nightclub business. The decline represents a secular trend that could turn out to be concerning for RCI Hospitality – young people are drinking less alcohol, as has for example recently been noted by Brown-Forman (BF.B). Spirits companies’ and many brewers’ sales trend already reflects the fact, and RCI Hospitality’s performance is now following. Lower alcohol consumption could continue to weigh on RCI Hospitality's alcohol sales, slowly pressuring traffic over the long term.

The Bombshells chain had a concerning quarter – same-store sales declined by -13.4%, halting some previous turnaround progress. The segment previously showed a much lower -7.5% decline in the metric in Q1, but Bombshells decline has now reaccelerated. Given that RCI Hospitality has recently divested five underperforming locations, but Q2 still showed such a sharp decline in comparable sales, the chain’s turnaround efforts seem very unsuccessful. Bombshells’ adjusted operating income declined to a negative -$0.1 million level from $0.8 million a year ago, as cost savings aren’t able to offset such a deep sales decline.

Notably, some of the Bombshells decline is clearly also temporary; as told, bad weather caused weak industry-wide restaurant traffic in early 2025. Yet, such a sharp decline in same-store sales hasn’t been reported by any peer that I know of – McDonald’s (MCD) -3.6% same-store sales decline in the period has been noted as very weak, still less than a third of Bombshells’.

RCI Hospitality is still developing Bombshells locations, with a Denver opening during Q2 and additional ones in Lubbock and Rowlett to open soon. Although no new developments seem likely after current construction, the new locations only add to a concerning segment outlook as traffic continues to plummet in Bombshells locations. RCI Hospitality targets Bombshells to recover to a 15% operating margin and positive same-store sales growth, but based on the current trend, I am skeptical of Bombshells’ future.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for RCI Hospitality’s stock to estimate a fair value. I now estimate slightly slower revenue growth than previously due to weaker same-store sales growth expectations, where RCI Hospitality now reaches $384.2 million in FY2029 revenues compared to the company’s $400 million target. The revenue estimates now suggest a 4.9% 2024-2034 CAGR through acquisitions and slow same-store sales growth, afterwards ending at stable 2% perpetual growth.

Due to RCI Hospitality’s weaker earnings performance, I now also estimate margins more cautiously. I estimate the EBIT margin to stay stagnant at a 18% level going forward, not suggesting a major earnings rebound for Bombshells.

I again factor expected nightclub acquisitions into cash flow estimates, weakening especially the next couple of years’ cash flow figures.

The estimates put RCI Hospitality’s fair value at $50.34, 20% above the stock price at the time of writing. The lower stock price still creates some upside in the valuation. Due to RCI Hospitality’s unclear earnings prospects, especially for Bombshells, though, I believe that the upside now merely represents a fair margin of safety – I don’t consider the lower price an attractive entry point. The stock is now cheap, mostly for good reason.

The fair value estimate is down from $70.82 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.42%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.71% interest rate, annualized from Q2 interest expenses and debt. I again assume quite a significant 40% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.38% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.13. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity stands at 11.17% and the WACC at 9.42%.

Takeaway

RCI Hospitality’s earnings are weakening. The nightclub segment’s performance has reflected several transitory weaknesses, but also a secular decline in alcohol consumption that looks to pressure the segment going forward as well. Bombshells’ turnaround isn’t progressing despite the divestment of underperforming locations. Due to weaker earnings expectations, I don’t believe the stock to be a clearly attractive investment anymore. As such, I now downgrade my rating on RCI Hospitality to Hold.

data.title: Unemployment Claims Unchanged From Previous Week, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Initial jobless claims measure the number of people who file for unemployment for the first time in a given week. In the week ending June 7th, initial jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 248,000, remaining at their highest level in eight months. This is unchanged from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was higher than the 242,000 forecast.

Here is the complete data series dating back to 1967. There is a good bit of volatility in this indicator, which is why the four-week moving average is a more useful number than the weekly data because it smooths short-term fluctuations to highlight the overall trend. The four-week moving average currently stands at 240,250 - up 5,000 from the previous week and the highest level in nine months.

Outside of the COVID spike, initial unemployment claims have never been greater than 700,000 for a given week, making the chart above less useful for identifying trends. To address this, we've adjusted the y-axis on the chart below to provide a closer view of the data, minimizing the impact of the COVID surge.

Notice the relationship between recessions and the rise in weekly unemployment claims. To no surprise, the 4-week moving average begins to rise at or before the start of a recession and peaks around its conclusion.

In another attempt to eliminate the COVID spike, the chart below shows initial unemployment claims starting in October 2021.

Initial Unemployment Claims: Not Seasonally Adjusted

The headline unemployment insurance data - and the charts above - are seasonally adjusted. But what does the non-seasonally adjusted data look like? The chart below highlights its extreme volatility, as shown by the green dots. The four-week moving average helps reveal the recurring seasonal patterns, such as the regular spikes in January.

Because of the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted weekly data, we can add a 52-week moving average to give a better sense of the secular trends. The chart below also has a linear regression through the data (red line), which we are currently below.

Continuing Unemployment Claims

Continuing unemployment claims are the number of people who have already filed for unemployment and have continued to claim benefits for a subsequent week. Unlike initial unemployment claims, continuing claims are not considered a leading economic indicator and are a less important measure of economic health.

Additionally, data for continuing claims has a one-week lag compared to initial claims. In the week ending May 31st, continuing jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 1,956,000, the highest level since November 2021. This represents an increase of 54,000 from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was higher than the 1,910,000 forecast.

Here is the data series dating back to 1967. Again, we've included the 4-week moving average to highlight the overall trend. The four-week moving average currently stands at 1,914,500 - an increase of 19,750 from the previous week and the highest level since November 2021.

Here is a zoomed in view so that the COVID spike is not as prominent.

And lastly, here is a chart starting in November 2021 to see the more recent trends in continuing claims.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boeing Has Clearer Skies Ahead But The Turnaround Is Not Over
data.description:
Kenio Fontes co-authored this article

Like a boxer that has survived the fight after being knocked down multiple times, Boeing (NYSE:BA) is still hanging in there. And unless the world order changes even more dramatically than it already has (and that's a lot!), BA should continue to be the most prominent aerospace company west of the Atlantic Ocean.

There are many reasons to feel like the worst is over for BA. For the business, that is. The stock is still part of a broader market, one that operates very differently now than it used to. But we conclude that BA has potential to be one of those stocks we look back at 10 years from now and say that regardless of what the market did, it was competitive.

With that backdrop, here's the technical view, followed by the fundamental and business aspects of the BA thesis.

First, unless this company is going away, we can't help but notice that its 10-year annualized return is about as low as it has been. 2.6% a year for 10 years incorporates the impact of the pandemic, several highly visible safety issues, union problems and tariffs, and more. If the long-term past is a long-term prologue, this stock, which has not had a 10-year negative return, is showing signs of long-term seller exhaustion. And with 8-16% annualized compounded return as a past reference range, the upside outweighs the down here.

The technical picture is also encouraging, though there could be some bumps in the road. We first covered BA back on December 11, 2024. And while we can be stingy with buy ratings, this one was already short-term washed out. Now, while it is not nearly as attractive near-term as it was back then, its longer-term positioning has bypassed some crisis moments.

This very long-term (monthly) chart indicates to me that if BA can burst through that upcoming downtrend around $210-$215, it could be ready to be a leader within the Dow Industrials, of which it is 1 of 30 member stocks.

However, the reaction to the Air India crash may have delayed that departure through that higher level for a while. The shorter-term daily chart (below) indicates to me that there's the risk of the market re-assessing BA at above $200 a share. BA has bumped its noggin on the overhead bin, so to speak, and is now looking toppy near-term.

The Path Is Complex But Less Cloudy Now

On the fundamental side, the first thing we have to keep in mind is that Boeing is a complex operation, and operations impact fundamentals, which impact price. The challenges mentioned, such as safety issues, impacted the financials.

For instance, Q1 was an improvement in a number of indicators, but the company still posted negative free cash flow of $2.3 billion and an operating margin of 2.4%. The chart below not only proves that the financials are quite compressed, but also shows us something very important for the thesis: operating leverage.

Boeing is very dependent on higher volume in order to dilute its high costs. E.g., with 2019 revenue, the net income margin could reach 10%, but when production and demand fall due to a number of factors, this acts as a counter-effect, with operational deleveraging greatly impacting results.

This already gives us a main trigger: the normalization/increase in production. In Q1, it was mentioned that 737 production is gradually increasing, and the expectation is to reach 38 per month by 2025, and closer to the end of the year to reach 42 per month, as mentioned in the earnings call. Higher production means a higher margin and, consequently, an enhancement in the shareholder value being generated.

The backlog is another positive point in the thesis, with $460 billion for commercial airplanes and $62 billion for defense, space & security, which was increased after winning the F-47 contract.

This adds greater predictability, but only as a proxy for gross revenue for the coming years, since converting this into margin still depends on other factors such as optimizations, production ramp-up, and the like. A reasonable proxy is to imagine that by converting $80 billion per year in revenue, there are a little fewer than six years to explore this backlog for commercial airplanes, and roughly speaking, if the consolidated net margin were 6%, we could expect a net income of $4.8 billion per year.

The balance sheet, in my view, is a double-edged sword. As the chart below shows, the company has close to $30 billion in net debt. This is a very considerable amount, especially for a company that has had high cash burn in the last 12 months.

But looking on the bright side, in addition to making the thesis more complex and riskier, the debt also becomes a trigger. In the earnings call, it was mentioned that in addition to access to $10 billion in revolving credit facilities, Boeing also has an agreement to sell part of its digital aviation solutions business for a similar amount. All of these are triggers for reducing net debt and strengthening the balance sheet.

In short, the company will still have to prove that it can resume production, adjust its balance sheet, and return to the level of income it once delivered, but the signs are already positive enough to remove some of the (relevant) uncertainties from the thesis.

Valuation: Good For A Turnaround, But Not Great

Trying to estimate a fair price for companies that are in a turnaround is always tricky, and in addition to this disclaimer, it is necessary to mention that the uncertainty here is even higher due to cyclicality.

Right off the bat, when looking at the market cap, it is clear that the valuation here is not so great. In mid-2019, which was a period when the company was able to deliver excellent fundamentals, such as a free cash flow of $13 billion, the market cap was $240 billion, i.e., the price-to-FCF multiple was 18x.

Now, after the rebound, the market cap has reached $160 billion. Note that the fundamentals are nowhere near what they once were, but the market cap has nevertheless managed to return to a more appropriate level. This occurred due to the factors mentioned in the previous section; the company now has a slightly clearer path.

Therefore, I don't think we can call the valuation here great or believe that there is tremendous asymmetry. But still, with the triggers mentioned, there is potential for appreciation.

The EV-to-EBITDA, for example, is 37x. With a higher level of production, transforming the backlog into more and more revenue, the EBITDA margin should increase substantially. Balance sheet initiatives, on the other hand, can reduce net debt, i.e., they can decrease enterprise value. With higher EBITDA and lower EV, the multiple should compress, or the market cap may rise to compensate for these improvements, which is expected.

The Bottom Line

There are potential gains to be captured in BA stock. As the technical picture shows, with a normalization of the scenario, the stock should return to producing more decent gains. The valuation also shows that if the company normalizes production and continues to execute its initiatives, free cash flow may once again show considerable volumes, and this should translate into a higher market cap and/or a higher shareholder yield.

The problem is that although the triggers and the path are now clearer, it is still complex and presents significant challenges, especially in the short and medium term, while the long term seems clearer due to the strength of the brand, the company's positioning, and trends such as demand for aviation. Therefore, risk management is even more advisable here.
